name: Apple
notes: Northern CA varieties like Gravenstein arrive first in July. Fuji and Granny Smith peak in fall.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a plastic bag; keeps 1-2 months. Keep away from other produce — apples emit ethylene.

name: Apricot
notes: Blink-and-you-miss-it season. Blenheim variety is the holy grail — intensely fragrant, rarely ships well.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp, then refrigerate. Eat within 2-3 days of ripening.

name: Artichoke
notes: Castroville, CA is the artichoke capital of the world. Spring crop is larger and more tender than fall.
storage_tip: Refrigerate unwashed in a plastic bag for up to a week. Sprinkle with water to keep fresh.

name: Arugula
notes: Available year-round in CA but peppery flavor is best in cool weather. Bolts and turns bitter in summer heat.
storage_tip: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate. Best used within 3-4 days.

name: Asian Pear
notes: Crisp like an apple, fragrant like a pear. Unlike European pears, these are eaten firm, not soft.
storage_tip: Refrigerate immediately — keeps up to 3 months in the crisper drawer.

name: Asparagus
notes: CA Delta and Salinas Valley produce the best domestic asparagus. Thinner spears aren't younger — just from a younger plant.
storage_tip: Stand upright in a jar of water in the fridge, like flowers. Use within 3 days.

name: Avocado
notes: CA Hass peaks spring through summer. Mexican imports fill shelves year-round, peaking fall through spring.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp. Speed up in a paper bag with a banana. Refrigerate once ripe for 2-3 days.

name: Banana
notes: Always imported, always available. No meaningful peak — global supply chain keeps shelves stocked.
storage_tip: Room temp until ripe. Separate from the bunch to slow ripening. Fridge turns peel brown but fruit stays good.

name: Beet
notes: Grown year-round in CA. Golden beets are milder than red. Don't toss the greens — they're delicious sautéed.
storage_tip: Remove greens immediately (they draw moisture). Roots keep 2-3 weeks in the fridge.

name: Bell Pepper
notes: Red, yellow, and orange are just riper green peppers — sweeter and more nutritious. CA grows huge volumes.
storage_tip: Refrigerate whole and unwashed. Use within a week. Cut peppers last 2-3 days.

name: Blackberry
notes: Look for plump, deeply colored berries with no red drupelets. Red means underripe and sour.
storage_tip: Don't wash until ready to eat. Refrigerate in a single layer on paper towels. Use within 2-3 days.

name: Blood Orange
notes: Moro variety is the most common in CA — deep burgundy flesh with berry-like overtones. Color varies by cold exposure.
storage_tip: Room temp for a week, fridge for up to 2 weeks.

name: Blueberry
notes: CA coastal regions produce excellent berries. The white bloom on blueberries is natural and a sign of freshness.
storage_tip: Refrigerate dry and unwashed. Wash just before eating. Keeps about a week.

name: Bok Choy
notes: Cool-weather crop that's sweeter after a light frost. Baby bok choy is more tender for quick stir-fries.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within a week.

name: Broccoli
notes: CA grows 90%+ of US broccoli, mostly in Salinas Valley and Imperial Valley. Sweetest in cool months.
storage_tip: Refrigerate dry and unwashed. Use within 5 days — yellowing florets mean it's past prime.

name: Brussels Sprout
notes: Frost converts starches to sugars — winter sprouts are noticeably sweeter. Smaller ones are more tender.
storage_tip: Refrigerate unwashed on the stalk if possible. Loose sprouts keep about a week.

name: Cabbage
notes: Year-round in CA but best in cool months. Savoy cabbage is more delicate; napa is the go-to for Asian cooking.
storage_tip: Tightly wrapped in the fridge, cabbage lasts 2+ weeks. Cut surfaces oxidize — use quickly once sliced.

name: Cantaloupe
notes: San Joaquin Valley is cantaloupe country. Ripe ones have a sweet floral scent at the stem end and a slight give.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp. Once ripe, refrigerate and eat within 3 days. Cut melon keeps 2 days covered in fridge.

name: Carrot
notes: Year-round in CA but cold weather concentrates sugars. Farmers market carrots in winter are a revelation.
storage_tip: Remove greens. Store in a bag in the fridge crisper for 2-3 weeks.

name: Cauliflower
notes: CA dominates US production. Purple and Romanesco varieties appear at farmers markets fall through spring.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a loosely sealed bag. Use within a week.

name: Celery
notes: CA grows most US celery year-round. Inner stalks (the heart) are more tender and less fibrous.
storage_tip: Wrap in aluminum foil and refrigerate — keeps crisp for 2+ weeks.

name: Swiss Chard
notes: Rainbow chard is the same plant in different colors. Stems take longer to cook than leaves — start them first.
storage_tip: Wrap in damp paper towel in the fridge. Use within 5 days.

name: Cherry
notes: CA cherries are among the first domestic fruit of spring. Bing and Rainier are most popular. Short, precious season.
storage_tip: Refrigerate immediately, unwashed, with stems on. Use within a week.

name: Cherimoya
notes: Mark Twain called it the most delicious fruit known to man. Creamy custard texture with banana-pineapple flavor.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp until slightly soft. Refrigerate once ripe and eat within 2 days.

name: Chile Pepper
notes: CA grows jalapeños, serranos, Anaheims, and more. Summer heat produces hotter peppers.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a paper bag for up to 2 weeks. Freeze whole for long-term storage.

name: Collard Greens
notes: Like Brussels sprouts, sweetened by frost. Sturdier than kale — great for braising or as a wrap substitute.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a damp towel. Keeps about a week.

name: Corn
notes: Sugar converts to starch rapidly after picking. Same-day corn from a farmers market is a completely different food.
storage_tip: Eat the day you buy it. If you must store, keep in the husk in the fridge — use within 2 days.

name: Cucumber
notes: Persian cucumbers from CA are thinner-skinned and more flavorful than standard slicers. No need to peel.
storage_tip: Wrap individually in paper towel and refrigerate. Use within a week.

name: Date
notes: Medjool dates from the Coachella Valley are world-class. Fresh dates have a completely different caramel texture than dried.
storage_tip: Room temp for a month, fridge for 6 months, freezer for a year.

name: Dragon Fruit
notes: Red-fleshed varieties have more flavor than white. Look for bright, evenly colored skin without many brown spots.
storage_tip: Refrigerate ripe fruit for up to 5 days. Cut dragon fruit oxidizes quickly — eat immediately.

name: Eggplant
notes: Japanese and Chinese varieties are more tender with fewer seeds than globe eggplant. Skin should be taut and glossy.
storage_tip: Room temp if using within 2 days. Refrigerate loosely wrapped for up to a week.

name: Fava Bean
notes: A spring farmers market staple. Double-shelling is tedious but worth it — blanch, then pop beans from their skins.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in pods. Use within a few days — they toughen quickly.

name: Fennel
notes: Anise flavor mellows dramatically when roasted. The fronds make an excellent garnish or salad addition.
storage_tip: Refrigerate wrapped in a damp towel. Keeps about a week.

name: Fig
notes: Two crops: a small breba crop in June, then the main crop Aug–Sept. Black Mission and Kadota are CA classics.
storage_tip: Extremely perishable. Eat within 1-2 days. Handle gently — bruises quickly.

name: Garlic
notes: Green garlic appears at farmers markets in spring. Fresh-harvest garlic in summer is juicier and milder than stored.
storage_tip: Store whole bulbs in a cool, dry, dark place. Good for months. Don't refrigerate.

name: Grapefruit
notes: CA Oro Blanco is sweeter and less bitter than standard varieties. Heavier fruit = juicier fruit.
storage_tip: Room temp for a week, fridge for 2-3 weeks.

name: Grape (Table)
notes: CA produces 99% of US table grapes. New seedless varieties like Cotton Candy have transformed the category.
storage_tip: Refrigerate unwashed in a ventilated bag. Wash just before eating.

name: Green Bean
notes: French haricots verts are thinner and more tender. Beans should snap cleanly — if they bend, they're old.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within 5 days.

name: Guava
notes: Pink-fleshed varieties are sweeter. Ripe guavas smell powerfully fragrant from across the room.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp. Refrigerate when ripe — use within 2 days.

name: Kale
notes: Lacinato (Tuscan) kale is more tender than curly for salads. Frost-kissed winter kale is markedly sweeter.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a bag with a dry paper towel. Keeps about a week.

name: Kiwi
notes: CA kiwis come from the Central Valley. Gold kiwi (usually imported) is sweeter with smooth edible skin.
storage_tip: Firm kiwis ripen at room temp in a few days. Refrigerate ripe kiwis for up to 4 weeks.

name: Kumquat
notes: Eat whole — the sweet skin balances the tart flesh. Nagami variety is most common in CA.
storage_tip: Room temp for a few days, fridge for 2-3 weeks.

name: Leek
notes: The white and light green parts are what you eat. Split lengthwise and wash thoroughly — sand hides in the layers.
storage_tip: Refrigerate unwashed. Keeps 1-2 weeks.

name: Lemon
notes: Eureka lemons dominate CA production. Year-round availability but winter lemons are juicier and more aromatic.
storage_tip: Room temp for a week, fridge for a month. Zest before juicing to get both.

name: Meyer Lemon
notes: A lemon-mandarin hybrid — sweeter, more floral, thinner-skinned. The signature CA citrus. Excellent for baking.
storage_tip: Room temp for a week, fridge for up to a month.

name: Lettuce
notes: Salinas Valley is America's Salad Bowl. Peak crispness in cooler months. Summer heat makes lettuce bolt and turn bitter.
storage_tip: Wrap in a damp paper towel and bag. Keeps about a week.

name: Lime
notes: Nearly all US limes are imported from Mexico. Key limes are smaller, more aromatic, with more seeds.
storage_tip: Room temp for a week, fridge for 2-3 weeks. Roll on counter before juicing for maximum yield.

name: Loquat
notes: Often overlooked — grows abundantly in SoCal yards. Flavor is like a tangy apricot-plum. Brief farmers market season.
storage_tip: Very perishable. Eat within 2-3 days. Do not stack — they bruise easily.

name: Lychee
notes: Extremely short fresh season. The bumpy red shell peels easily to reveal translucent, perfumy flesh.
storage_tip: Refrigerate immediately. Keeps about a week. Shell darkens in fridge but fruit inside stays good.

name: Mandarin / Tangerine
notes: Satsumas arrive first in November, followed by Clementines and Murcotts. CA mandarins are some of the world's best.
storage_tip: Room temp for a week, fridge for 2-3 weeks.

name: Mango
notes: Mexican Ataulfo (honey) mangoes are supremely creamy. Don't judge ripeness by color — judge by squeeze and aroma.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp. Refrigerate once ripe for 3-5 days.

name: Mushroom
notes: Cultivated varieties (button, cremini, shiitake) are year-round. Wild chanterelles and porcini appear in fall/winter rains.
storage_tip: Store in a paper bag in the fridge. Never seal in plastic — they get slimy.

name: Navel Orange
notes: CA's flagship citrus. The navel is actually a conjoined twin orange. Best for eating; Valencia is better for juicing.
storage_tip: Room temp for a week, fridge for 2-3 weeks.

name: Valencia Orange
notes: The juicing orange. Green patches on skin in summer are normal (regreening) and don't affect quality or flavor.
storage_tip: Room temp for a week, fridge for several weeks.

name: Nectarine
notes: Genetically a smooth-skinned peach, not a peach-plum hybrid. White-fleshed varieties are sweeter but less tangy.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp stem-end down. Refrigerate once ripe — use within 3 days.

name: Onion
notes: Spring onions at farmers markets are milder and sweeter. Stockton Red onions are a CA specialty in late spring.
storage_tip: Store whole onions in a cool, dry, dark place. Cut onions: wrap tightly and fridge for a few days.

name: Papaya
notes: Maradol (Mexican) papayas are large with salmon-pink flesh. Solo papayas from Hawaii are smaller and sweeter.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp until skin is mostly yellow and fruit gives slightly. Refrigerate ripe for 2-3 days.

name: Parsnip
notes: Needs frost to convert starch to sugar — winter parsnips are dramatically sweeter. Roast to caramelize.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a bag. Keeps 2-3 weeks.

name: Passion Fruit
notes: Wrinkled skin means ripe, not old. The more shriveled, the sweeter. Intensely aromatic pulp with edible seeds.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp until wrinkled. Refrigerate ripe fruit for up to a week.

name: Peach
notes: CA grows over 50% of US peaches. Freestone varieties are best for eating; clingstone for canning. Smell the stem end.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp stem-end down. Once ripe, refrigerate and eat within 3 days.

name: Pear
notes: Pears ripen off the tree — buy firm and ripen at home. Bartlett changes color when ripe; Bosc and Anjou don't.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp. Check the neck — when it yields to gentle pressure, it's ready. Refrigerate ripe pears.

name: Pea (Snap/Snow/English)
notes: Sugar snaps are the total package — eat pod and all. English peas need shelling but have the sweetest flavor.
storage_tip: Refrigerate immediately — sugars convert to starch fast. Best eaten same day.

name: Persimmon
notes: Fuyu (squat) can be eaten firm like an apple. Hachiya (acorn-shaped) must be fully soft or it's painfully astringent.
storage_tip: Fuyu: room temp until slightly soft. Hachiya: room temp until very soft and jelly-like.

name: Pineapple
notes: Year-round from Costa Rica but sweetest spring through summer. Pineapples don't ripen after picking — buy golden and fragrant.
storage_tip: Room temp 1-2 days. Cut pineapple keeps 3-4 days refrigerated in an airtight container.

name: Plum
notes: CA grows over 75 plum varieties. Santa Rosa and Elephant Heart are classics. Pluots are plum-apricot hybrids.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp. Refrigerate once ripe — use within 3-4 days.

name: Pomegranate
notes: Wonderful variety dominates CA production. Heavier = juicier. Score and break apart in water to avoid staining.
storage_tip: Whole fruit keeps 1-2 months in fridge. Seeds keep a week refrigerated or freeze for months.

name: Pomelo
notes: The original citrus — grapefruit's ancestor. Milder and sweeter with thick pithy rind. Great in salads.
storage_tip: Room temp for a week, fridge for 2 weeks.

name: Potato
notes: New potatoes (spring) have thin, flaky skin and creamy texture. Year-round from storage but spring digs are special.
storage_tip: Cool, dark, dry place — never refrigerate (converts starch to sugar). Keep away from onions.

name: Pumpkin
notes: Sugar pie pumpkins are for eating — those big jack-o'-lantern pumpkins are watery and flavorless.
storage_tip: Whole pumpkins keep for months in a cool, dry place.

name: Radicchio
notes: Bitterness mellows dramatically when grilled or roasted. Treviso is the elongated variety — slightly less bitter.
storage_tip: Refrigerate wrapped. Keeps about 2 weeks.

name: Radish
notes: Quick-growing spring crop. Watermelon radishes have stunning green-to-pink interiors. French breakfast radishes with butter and salt are perfect.
storage_tip: Remove greens. Submerge in water in the fridge for maximum crispness. Keeps 1-2 weeks.

name: Raspberry
notes: CA coastal climate is ideal. Golden raspberries are milder and sweeter. Inspect containers for mold at the bottom.
storage_tip: Don't wash until eating. Use within 2 days. Single-layer on paper towel in fridge.

name: Rhubarb
notes: Only eat the stalks — leaves are toxic. Field-grown is tart and intense; hothouse is milder. Pairs perfectly with strawberries.
storage_tip: Refrigerate wrapped in a damp towel. Keeps about 2 weeks. Freezes well chopped.

name: Rutabaga
notes: Sweeter than turnips, especially after frost. Excellent mashed, roasted, or added to stews.
storage_tip: Refrigerate. Keeps several weeks.

name: Spinach
notes: Year-round in CA but spring spinach is sweeter and more tender. Bolts in summer heat.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a bag with a paper towel. Use within 5 days.

name: Summer Squash / Zucchini
notes: Smaller is better — large zucchini are watery and seedy. Blossoms are a delicacy, stuffed and fried.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within a week.

name: Winter Squash
notes: Butternut, delicata, kabocha, acorn — each has distinct character. Delicata skin is edible. Flavor improves with storage.
storage_tip: Store whole in a cool, dry place for months. Cut squash keeps a week in the fridge.

name: Strawberry
notes: CA grows 90% of US strawberries. Available almost year-round but spring berries are a different universe of flavor.
storage_tip: Don't wash until eating. Refrigerate on paper towels. Use within 2-3 days.

name: Sunchoke (Jerusalem Artichoke)
notes: Nutty, slightly sweet, with a crisp water chestnut texture when raw. Roast until caramelized for the best experience.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a paper bag. Keeps 2-3 weeks.

name: Sweet Potato
notes: Garnet and Jewel varieties dominate CA. True yams are a different plant entirely — what Americans call yams are sweet potatoes.
storage_tip: Store in a cool, dark place — never refrigerate raw (damages texture). Keeps several weeks.

name: Tomatillo
notes: The papery husk peels off to reveal a sticky, tart fruit essential for salsa verde. Smaller ones tend to be sweeter.
storage_tip: Leave husks on. Room temp for a week, fridge for 2-3 weeks.

name: Tomato
notes: Peak CA heirlooms in August are one of the great food experiences. Never refrigerate a tomato — it kills flavor and texture.
storage_tip: Always room temp, stem-end down. Eat within a few days of ripening.

name: Turnip
notes: Young turnips are mild and sweet. Hakurei (Japanese) turnips are small, white, and can be eaten raw like radishes.
storage_tip: Remove greens. Refrigerate roots in a bag for 2-3 weeks.

name: Watermelon
notes: Look for a pronounced yellow ground spot and hollow sound when tapped. Seedless varieties dominate but seeded have more flavor.
storage_tip: Whole: room temp. Cut: refrigerate and eat within 3-4 days.

name: Cactus Pear (Prickly Pear)
notes: Vibrant magenta flesh, watermelon-bubblegum flavor. Handle with care — tiny invisible glochids are devilish.
storage_tip: Refrigerate for up to a week. Use tongs or thick gloves when handling.

name: Tangelo
notes: Minneola tangelos have a distinctive knob at the stem. Very juicy, tangy-sweet — a grapefruit-tangerine cross.
storage_tip: Room temp for a week, fridge for 2-3 weeks.

name: Kohlrabi
notes: Tastes like a mild, sweet broccoli stem. Excellent raw, shredded into slaws, or roasted. Peel the tough outer skin.
storage_tip: Remove leaves. Refrigerate for 2-3 weeks.

name: Romanesco
notes: Nature's fractal — a psychedelic relative of cauliflower. Nuttier, more delicate flavor. Stunning in presentation.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within a week.

name: Olive (Fresh)
notes: Fresh olives are inedible raw — they must be cured. CA olive oil harvest peaks in fall. Mission and Manzanilla are key CA varieties.
storage_tip: Process immediately if curing. Olive oil: store in dark, cool place.

name: Pluot / Plumcot
notes: A plum-apricot hybrid — more plum than apricot. Flavor Grenade and Dapple Dandy are CA favorites. Extremely sweet when ripe.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp. Refrigerate once ripe — use within 3 days.

name: Celery Root (Celeriac)
notes: Ugly but delicious — intense celery flavor. Classic in French rémoulade or pureed into soup.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a bag. Keeps 2-3 weeks.

name: Broccoli Rabe (Rapini)
notes: Not actually broccoli — more related to turnips. Pleasantly bitter. Quick blanch before sautéing tames the bite.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a damp towel. Use within 3-4 days.

name: Honeydew Melon
notes: Often unfairly maligned — a good honeydew is floral and honey-sweet. Should feel slightly waxy, not rock-hard.
storage_tip: Ripen at room temp. Refrigerate once ripe. Cut melon keeps 2-3 days.

name: Almond (Fresh)
notes: CA produces 80% of the world's almonds. Fresh green almonds in spring are a brief farmers market delicacy — tart and crunchy.
storage_tip: Fresh almonds: refrigerate, use within days. Dried: cool, dark place for months.

name: Walnut (Fresh)
notes: Fresh-harvest walnuts in fall are milder and less bitter than stored. CA grows 99% of US walnuts.
storage_tip: Shell and refrigerate or freeze. In-shell keeps months in a cool place.

name: Green Garlic
notes: Young garlic harvested before the bulb forms. Mild, green-onion-like with a gentle garlic warmth. A spring farmers market treasure.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within a week.

name: Cactus Pad (Nopales)
notes: Tangy, green-bean-like flavor. De-spine, slice, and grill or sauté until the slime cooks off. Essential in Mexican cuisine.
storage_tip: Refrigerate in a bag. Use within a week.
